query: execution bottlenecks complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Improve Workflows by Managing Bottlenecks
results.content: Bottlenecks are a common source of frustration in organizations. A company’s legal review process may delay the execution of high-value contracts, or a shortage of computing resources may slow progress on a new digital initiative. Such constraints can cause tasks to pile up and hinder teams’ abilities to move forward with their work, costing companies time and money. When leaders encounter a bottleneck, they may dedicate resources to addressing it only to find that the process in question is still stalled by other bottlenecks. Our research has found that organizational bottlenecks can be best managed or avoided not by addressing them piecemeal but by taking a holistic view of work systems and resource portfolios and aligning them in ways that improve organizational performance. [...] Bottlenecks manifest as tasks that are stalled for one of two reasons: because they depend on the output of other tasks that have not been completed, or because the resources required to complete the task are not available. Task bottlenecks frequently occur as teams wait for approvals from legal or compliance departments, for example. Resource bottlenecks happen when there is a lack of resources necessary to complete a task or process — say, if a construction project has only one crane available and there are competing demands to use the crane.

results.title: Celonis Study Finds that Rigid Systems and Broken Processes Hamper Business Execution
results.content:
In accounts payable and accounts receivable, respondents cited rigid systems and technologies as their biggest obstacle to performing at their full execution capacity.  
Execution capacity is the maximum level of performance a company can achieve with its available time and resources. Those surveyed called out broken or inefficient processes as a top 3 obstacle for accounts payable, accounts receivable, and procurement.  
The other root causes of execution barriers identified in the research include: [...] Business leaders blame rigid systems and technologies as well as broken or inefficient processes for creating execution gaps that damage their business performance, according to the “2021 State of Business Execution Benchmark Report,” released today by Celonis.  
The report, based on an independent survey of 2,000 business leaders commissioned by Celonis, found significant execution gaps between average and best-in-class performance in core business functions of accounts payable (AP), accounts receivable (AR), procurement and order management that can cost companies hundreds of millions of dollars in missed opportunities. [...] ## The execution capacity problem

Despite making massive digital investments to transform their business operations, companies continue to face costly execution barriers. By identifying and fixing these problems, they have the opportunity to reach the execution capacity levels of the top-performing companies.  
Key performance differences highlighted by the research include:

Accounts Payable

 On average companies pay on time only 50% of the time, compared to 77% for top performers
 Processing a single invoice costs $17.42 on average, while top performers reduced this cost by more than half to $6.84

Accounts Receivable

results.title: How Leaders Unknowingly Make Themselves the Bottleneck
results.content:
Understanding how accountability breaks down at scale is the next leadership challenge, and it’s where execution either holds or quietly unravels.

If execution feels harder than it should, don’t start by tightening accountability. Instead, start by strengthening clarity.

Slow decisions, endless meetings, broken handoffs, hidden problems and lost learning all trace back to the same source. The system hasn’t made judgment, ownership and expectations clear enough.

When leaders take responsibility for clarity, accountability stops feeling heavy. Execution starts to move.

Sign up for the Entrepreneur Daily newsletter to get the news and resources you need to know today to help you run your business better. Get it in your inbox.

## Key Takeaways [...] You're viewing the US website. Looking for the Site edition?

# Does Execution Feel Harder Than It Should? Eliminate This Hidden Friction That’s Keeping Your Team Stuck.

You don’t have an execution problem. You have a clarity problem.

Copied to clipboard

Opinions expressed by Entrepreneur contributors are their own.

## Key Takeaways

You’ve hired smart people, and you’ve invested in tools. You’ve also restructured more than once. Given all these, on paper, everything should work.

But the reality is different.

Decisions take longer than they should, and ownership gets blurred. Teams move, then stall, then circle back. You step in more than you want to, not because you enjoy it, but because progress depends on you doing so.

## Why execution pain gets mislabeled as accountability [...] ## Why execution pain gets mislabeled as accountability

Most leaders describe this as “an accountability issue.” They believe people aren’t owning outcomes, follow-through is inconsistent, and as a result, things fall through the cracks.

What I’ve learned over decades of advising senior leaders is different.

Accountability rarely fails on its own. It fails because clarity was never strong enough to support it.

When expectations are vague, accountability becomes personal. When ownership is unclear, follow-up feels like policing. Leaders sense this and hesitate, which only makes the problem worse.

## Clarity is not communication

Many leaders respond by explaining things again. They send follow-ups, restate priorities and assume repetition will create alignment.

It doesn’t.

results.title: Break Decision Rules to End Execution Bottleneck | Chandell Labbozzetta posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
One of the most expensive assumptions in business is:
“People are the problem.”
When performance drops, that’s often where leaders look first.
The salesperson isn’t performing.
The manager isn’t holding people accountable.
The production team isn’t moving fast enough.
The installer made a mistake.
Sometimes that’s true.
But more often, I find people are operating exactly as the system is designed.
If priorities are unclear, people will make different decisions.
If metrics are missing, people will create their own scorecards.
If accountability is inconsistent, behaviors will be inconsistent.
If the process is broken, even great people will struggle.
One of the lessons engineering taught me is this:
Before changing the people, examine the system. [...] Good systems reduce questions.
Good systems make the right action obvious.
Most teams aren't failing because they're lazy.
They're struggling because they're navigating operational chaos.
And no amount of accountability can fix a broken process.
What's one process in your business that creates more confusion than clarity?
🎥 @bbooster\_usa [...] To view or add a comment, sign in

View profile for Ivan Ting, MPM-PPPA

Most overwhelmed leaders do not have a productivity problem.
They have a control problem.
They keep saying:
“I’ll just do it myself.”
Because it feels faster.
Safer.
Higher quality.
But over time…
That habit trains the team to stop taking ownership.
Then leaders complain:
“Why does nobody step up?”
Because ownership cannot grow in environments where leaders unconsciously reclaim control every time mistakes happen.
That’s the trap.
You think you’re protecting standards.
But slowly… you’re teaching dependency.
And dependency destroys scalability.

results.title: What are Bottlenecks in Business & How to Solve Them | Hyperdrive Agile
results.content:
The solution lies in better data analysis, crystal-clear ownership assignments, and regular workflow reviews that actually lead to optimized processes and fewer bottlenecks plaguing your future projects.

### 4. Resource Bottlenecks

Sometimes, the bottleneck comes down to a simple (but frustrating) reality: you just don’t have what you need.

Whether it’s raw materials, equipment that actually works, or skilled team members who know what they’re doing, these resource constraints can stop you dead in your tracks. Supply chain disruptions, equipment failures that seem to happen at the worst possible moment, or staff shortages can leave you dealing with excess inventory or production stages that have ground to a complete halt. [...] The result? Product leaders have to continually deal with reduced production efficiency and customers who aren’t exactly thrilled with what they’re getting. Your best bet for tackling these business bottlenecks? Upgrading machinery, modernizing your roadmaps with agile methods, and tightening up that supply chain management.

### 3. Project Management Bottlenecks

Ever feel like your team is speaking different languages? When project management processes are about as clear as mud or communication bottlenecks start building walls between team members, delays become inevitable. Poor project planning, resource allocation that misses the mark, or training gaps can transform what should be manageable complex tasks into bottlenecks that just won’t quit. [...] Business agility aligns strategy, budgeting, and execution so constraints surface early and get removed quickly. It combines a few disciplines: OKR-driven alignment, product-centric ownership, visual flow management, and business leadership cadence for fast decisions.

This approach is valuable in every organization, whether you’re in the business of marketing consumer goods, helping discover new energy sources, or sending spaceships to Mars!

The goal is simple: optimize end-to-end flow and make constraint removal a continuous habit.

### 1. Visualize Work and Establish Cadence

The use of visual workflows, such as value stream mapping or flowcharting, provides a clear overview of each stage in the process, helping teams analyze performance and locate issues effectively.
